Case Studies

All documented
evaluations

CASE-0012025
RLHF · Adversarial Testing
Constraint & Instruction Hierarchy Failures
Problem
Operator-layer constraints overridden by user-layer preferences. Surface fluency masked the failure. Standard raters passed outputs violating explicit exclusions buried in system context. Cascade attribution: downstream failures traced to the first violated constraint.
Method
Constraint hierarchy analysis — explicit exclusions evaluated before surrounding response quality. Documented 6 failure archetypes with reproducible prompt conditions for each. Applied cascade attribution to trace secondary failures to primary constraint violations.
Outcome
6 failure archetypes documented. Adversarial test battery built across all categories. Constraint violations that were invisible to prior raters identified and isolated.
Outputs failing explicit exclusion constraints are rejected regardless of response quality elsewhere. Fluency does not rehabilitate a violated exclusion.
Prevented constraint-violating outputs from entering downstream preference datasets and corrupting RLHF training signal.
Adversarial TestingConstraint HierarchyRLHF RankingFailure TaxonomyCascade Attribution
6failure archetypes
0escalations
CASE-0022024
RLHF · Hallucination Detection
Plausible-Sounding Fabrication Detection
Problem
Structurally convincing responses with embedded subtly fabricated citations and distorted statistics. Correct format, confident tone. Surface-level raters scored them highly. Failure entirely invisible to keyword-scan evaluation.
Method
Entailed vs compatible test: claims entailed by the task are valid; claims merely compatible with it are hallucination flags. Built 4-type taxonomy: statistical distortion, false attribution, entity fabrication, confident speculation. One confirmed hallucination overrides everything else.
Outcome
30+ recurring failure patterns documented across workflows. Failure classes entirely invisible to prior evaluation passes. Escalated with reproducible prompt conditions for targeted fine-tuning intervention.
Confident tone is not evidence. Any precise claim not directly supported by the prompt triggers explicit reality check — one confirmed hallucination is an automatic reject.
Prevented hallucinated outputs from entering training datasets, protecting downstream model behaviour from corrupted preference signal.
Hallucination DetectionFact VerificationPattern AnalysisRLHF RankingFailure Taxonomy
30+failure patterns
0escalations
CASE-0052025
Multimodal · Image-Text Alignment
Image-Text Alignment & Visual Grounding Failures
Problem
Models generating fluent, contextually coherent image descriptions that misrepresented specific visual details, relationships, and spatial arrangements. Invisible to text-only reviewers. Fluency-accuracy correlation is lower in multimodal outputs than text-only tasks.
Method
Dual-layer evaluation protocol: text coherence evaluated independently from visual grounding accuracy. Built mismatch detection taxonomy: entity misidentification, spatial relationship errors, attribute hallucination, cross-modal confidence masking.
Outcome
Systematic visual grounding failure patterns identified across object relationships and scene understanding. Established that text-only evaluation rubrics are insufficient for multimodal outputs. Dual-layer protocol adopted for subsequent evaluation cycles.
Textual fluency in multimodal outputs is an unreliable quality proxy. Each visual claim requires independent grounding verification.
Dual-layer verification protocol adopted across subsequent multimodal evaluation cycles, preventing visually inaccurate outputs from passing text-only evaluation.
Multimodal EvaluationVisual GroundingImage-Text AlignmentHallucination DetectionDual-Layer Protocol
Dual-layerprotocol developed
0escalations
CASE-0032024
Bias Detection · Cross-Cultural QA
Quantifying the Africa Knowledge Gap
Problem
Model responses about African institutions, geography, and cultural context were systematically lower quality than equivalent Western responses. The gap was invisible to US-centric rubrics — a meta-bias in the evaluation itself, distinct from model bias.
Method
Parallel prompt battery matched across Western and African contexts in information density and complexity. Applied identical rubrics to both sets. Tracked quality variance by region, knowledge domain, and question type. Documented evaluator bias as a separate failure mode.
Outcome
Quantified 3.3-point average quality gap (out of 10) correlated with training data distribution. First structured evidence of this gap in the evaluation pipeline. Directly informed data collection prioritisation for the affected domains.
A 3.3-point gap on matched prompts is not noise — it is a systematic training data failure. Flagged as a data collection priority, not an output quality issue.
Structured evidence directly informed data collection strategy for underrepresented knowledge domains, addressing the training gap at the source.
Bias DetectionCross-Cultural QAParallel Prompt DesignRubric DesignAfrica Knowledge Gap
−3.3 ptsmeasured quality gap
0escalations
CASE-0042023
Data Ops · Schema Validation
50K-Point Schema Normalisation
Problem
AI training dataset accumulated silent schema drift across batches from multiple annotation sources. No hard errors — inconsistent labelling conventions that would corrupt model signal downstream, with no surface indicator that anything was wrong.
Method
Cross-batch consistency audit mapping every deviation category. Identified schema drift vectors. Produced normalisation guide aligning all batches to single standard with documented correction rules per deviation type. Per-entry accuracy insufficient — cross-batch consistency required.
Outcome
99%+ accuracy across all 50,000+ entries. Audit documentation accepted without revision through multiple QA cycles. Zero post-delivery escalations. Reusable audit framework applied to subsequent annotation batches.
Schema drift with no surface errors is critical failure — it is silent corruption of training signal. Pass threshold requires cross-batch consistency, not just per-entry accuracy.
Silent schema corruption removed before it could degrade downstream model training signal. Audit framework reused across subsequent annotation batches.
99%+ AccuracySchema AuditAudit-Ready DocsPipeline QCCross-Batch Validation
50,000+entries · 99%+ accuracy
0post-delivery escalations
Annotation Samples

Sample evaluation
outputs

Illustrative examples of the evaluation output format. Decision visible in the lead sentence. No rubric language.

RLHF · Preference Ranking
Prompt (simplified)
"Explain how neural networks learn, without using the word 'gradient' or 'backpropagation'."
Model Response Summary
Response explained how networks adjust weights using "gradient descent through backpropagation" in the third sentence. Otherwise well-structured and accurate.
Response violates explicit exclusion constraints — "backpropagation" and "gradient" appear in sentence 3. The rest of the response quality is irrelevant: explicit exclusion constraint failure is a hard reject regardless of surrounding quality.
Hallucination Detection
Prompt (simplified)
"Summarise the key findings of recent studies on transformer attention mechanisms."
Model Response Summary
Response cited "Vaswani et al. (2023)" twice as a recent study, attributing specific findings to it. No 2023 paper by Vaswani et al. exists — the original Attention Is All You Need paper is from 2017.
Response contains a confirmed hallucination: false citation attributed to a non-existent paper. The fabricated attribution overrides all other quality signals — one confirmed hallucination is a hard reject. The correct paper date is 2017, not 2023.
Instruction Following
Prompt (simplified)
"Write a 3-bullet executive summary of this product launch. Each bullet must be a single sentence."
Model Response Summary
Response produced exactly 3 bullets. Each bullet was a single sentence. Content was accurate and concise relative to the provided text.
Response satisfies all explicit constraints: bullet count, sentence-per-bullet requirement, and content accuracy. No hallucinations detected. No ambiguity introduced. Meets the pass threshold.
Multimodal · Visual Grounding
Prompt (simplified)
"Describe what is happening in the image." [Image shows two people seated at a table with food.]
Model Response Summary
Response described "a person standing at a counter preparing food." The image shows two people seated at a table. The spatial relationship and number of subjects are both wrong.
Visual grounding failure: response misrepresents two subjects as one, seated as standing, and table as counter. Fluent and well-written, but visual accuracy is independently evaluated from text quality. This is a multimodal hard fail.
